entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Mechanized toy robot with working arms and crude eyesight; requires precise eye alignment to receive visual flash signals from NES screen; moves slowly and deliberately; requires player to plan moves ahead to allow R.O.B. to execute actions in synchronized rhythm

notes: Stack-Up is a puzzle game designed as a companion piece to the R.O.B. robot peripheral. Unlike traditional video games with world-state danger/safety signaling, faction systems, or political narratives, Stack-Up communicates primarily through physical robot-screen interaction mechanics rather than visual world-state manifestation. The game's 'communication' occurs through: (1) NES screen flashing patterns that R.O.B.'s infrared sensors detect, (2) visual feedback showing R.O.B.'s arm movements responding to on-screen puzzle states, (3) synchronized visual-to-physical feedback loop where screen shows block stacking goals and R.O.B. physically manipulates blocks. No danger/safety signals, faction identities, or world-state metaphors documented. The game is primarily a hardware demonstration and toy accessory experience rather than a narrative or world-driven experience.
